# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

If a decision is necessary and cannot be discovered from local context, ask the user directly. However, in Default mode you should strongly prefer executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions.

payload.user_instructions:
<!-- BEGIN COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->
## Compound Codex Tool Mapping (Claude Compatibility)

This section maps Claude Code plugin tool references to Codex behavior.
Only this block is managed automatically.

Tool mapping:
- Read: use shell reads (cat/sed) or rg
- Write: create files via shell redirection or apply_patch
- Edit/MultiEdit: use apply_patch
- Bash: use shell_command
- Grep: use rg (fallback: grep)
- Glob: use rg --files or find
- LS: use ls via shell_command
- WebFetch/WebSearch: use curl or Context7 for library docs
- AskUserQuestion/Question: ask the user in chat
- Task/Subagent/Parallel: run sequentially in main thread; use multi_tool_use.parallel for tool calls
- TodoWrite/TodoRead: use file-based todos in todos/ with file-todos skill
- Skill: open the referenced SKILL.md and follow it
- ExitPlanMode: ignore
<!-- END COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->

--- project-doc ---

# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
- `apps/` contains product apps (e.g., `apps/org`, `apps/donor`, `apps/builder`, `apps/storybook`).
- `packages/` contains shared packages like `packages/ui`, `packages/utils`, `packages/tokens`, and config.
- Shared scripts live in `scripts/`, repo config in `config/`, and tooling in `turbo.json` + `biome.jsonc`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
- `pnpm dev` starts the local dev experience via `./scripts/dev`.
- `pnpm build` runs `turbo build` for all packages/apps.
- `pnpm test` runs `turbo test` (Vitest in each workspace).
- `pnpm typecheck` runs `turbo typecheck`.
- `pnpm lint` / `pnpm lint:fix` run Biome checks and auto-fixes.
- `pnpm storybook` starts Storybook (`apps/storybook`).
- `pnpm test:storybook` runs Storybook tests.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
- Indentation: 2 spaces (see `biome.jsonc`).
- Prefer semantic design tokens and Tailwind utility variables from `packages/ui` (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- File naming: use `kebab-case` for folders, `kebab-case.tsx` for components, and `*.test.ts(x)` for tests.
- Run Biome before pushing: `pnpm lint`.

## Testing Guidelines
- Testing framework: Vitest (see `vitest.config.ts` in apps/packages).
- Name tests as `*.test.ts` or `*.test.tsx` near the source module.
- Run all tests with `pnpm test`, or target a workspace with `pnpm --filter <pkg> test`.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
- Commit messages follow Conventional Commits: `type(scope): subject` (example: `feat(sidebar): add collapse`).
- Commits must be GPG signed (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- Branch naming: `[username]/[feature-description]`.
- PRs should include: concise summary, linked issue (if any), test notes, and screenshots for UI changes.

## Security & Configuration Tips
- Use the setup script for local SSL/hosts setup: `./scripts/setup.sh` (see `SETUP.md`).
- Keep `node` and `pnpm` aligned with `package.json` engines (`node >= 24.6.0`, `pnpm >= 10.6.4`).


# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
- Source lives in `src/`, with entrypoints and app code in TypeScript/React.
- Static assets live in `public/`.
- Build output is generated into `dist/` (do not edit by hand).
- App configuration is in `vite.config.js`, `tsconfig.json`, and `tailwind.config.ts`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
- `pnpm dev`: start the local dev server via `./scripts/dev` (from repo root).
- `pnpm build`: run `turbo build` for all apps/packages.
- `pnpm test`: run `turbo test` (Vitest per workspace).
- `pnpm typecheck`: run `turbo typecheck`.
- `pnpm lint` / `pnpm lint:fix`: run Biome checks and auto-fixes.
- `pnpm storybook`: start Storybook (`apps/storybook`).
- `pnpm test:storybook`: run Storybook tests.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
- Indentation: 2 spaces (see `biome.jsonc`).
- Prefer semantic design tokens and Tailwind utilities from `packages/ui`.
- File naming: `kebab-case` for folders, `kebab-case.tsx` for components.
- Tests: `*.test.ts` or `*.test.tsx` next to the source module.

## Testing Guidelines
- Framework: Vitest (see each app/package `vitest.config.ts`).
- Name tests `*.test.ts(x)` and keep them close to the code they cover.
- Run all tests with `pnpm test`, or scope with `pnpm --filter <pkg> test`.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
- Commit messages follow Conventional Commits: `type(scope): subject`.
  - Example: `feat(sidebar): add collapse`.
- Commits must be GPG signed (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- Branch naming: `[username]/[feature-description]`.
- PRs should include: concise summary, linked issue (if any), test notes,
  and screenshots for UI changes.

## Security & Configuration Tips
- Use `./scripts/setup.sh` for local SSL/hosts setup (see `SETUP.md`).
- Keep `node` and `pnpm` aligned with `package.json` engines:
  `node >= 24.6.0`, `pnpm >= 10.6.4`.

### How to use skills
- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.
  3) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  4) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  5) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.
  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.

settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

If a decision is necessary and cannot be discovered from local context, ask the user directly. However, in Default mode you should strongly prefer executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions.

payload.user_instructions:
<!-- BEGIN COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->
## Compound Codex Tool Mapping (Claude Compatibility)

This section maps Claude Code plugin tool references to Codex behavior.
Only this block is managed automatically.

Tool mapping:
- Read: use shell reads (cat/sed) or rg
- Write: create files via shell redirection or apply_patch
- Edit/MultiEdit: use apply_patch
- Bash: use shell_command
- Grep: use rg (fallback: grep)
- Glob: use rg --files or find
- LS: use ls via shell_command
- WebFetch/WebSearch: use curl or Context7 for library docs
- AskUserQuestion/Question: ask the user in chat
- Task/Subagent/Parallel: run sequentially in main thread; use multi_tool_use.parallel for tool calls
- TodoWrite/TodoRead: use file-based todos in todos/ with file-todos skill
- Skill: open the referenced SKILL.md and follow it
- ExitPlanMode: ignore
<!-- END COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->

--- project-doc ---

# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
- `apps/` contains product apps (e.g., `apps/org`, `apps/donor`, `apps/builder`, `apps/storybook`).
- `packages/` contains shared packages like `packages/ui`, `packages/utils`, `packages/tokens`, and config.
- Shared scripts live in `scripts/`, repo config in `config/`, and tooling in `turbo.json` + `biome.jsonc`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
- `pnpm dev` starts the local dev experience via `./scripts/dev`.
- `pnpm build` runs `turbo build` for all packages/apps.
- `pnpm test` runs `turbo test` (Vitest in each workspace).
- `pnpm typecheck` runs `turbo typecheck`.
- `pnpm lint` / `pnpm lint:fix` run Biome checks and auto-fixes.
- `pnpm storybook` starts Storybook (`apps/storybook`).
- `pnpm test:storybook` runs Storybook tests.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
- Indentation: 2 spaces (see `biome.jsonc`).
- Prefer semantic design tokens and Tailwind utility variables from `packages/ui` (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- File naming: use `kebab-case` for folders, `kebab-case.tsx` for components, and `*.test.ts(x)` for tests.
- Run Biome before pushing: `pnpm lint`.

## Testing Guidelines
- Testing framework: Vitest (see `vitest.config.ts` in apps/packages).
- Name tests as `*.test.ts` or `*.test.tsx` near the source module.
- Run all tests with `pnpm test`, or target a workspace with `pnpm --filter <pkg> test`.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
- Commit messages follow Conventional Commits: `type(scope): subject` (example: `feat(sidebar): add collapse`).
- Commits must be GPG signed (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- Branch naming: `[username]/[feature-description]`.
- PRs should include: concise summary, linked issue (if any), test notes, and screenshots for UI changes.

## Security & Configuration Tips
- Use the setup script for local SSL/hosts setup: `./scripts/setup.sh` (see `SETUP.md`).
- Keep `node` and `pnpm` aligned with `package.json` engines (`node >= 24.6.0`, `pnpm >= 10.6.4`).


# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
- Source lives in `src/`, with entrypoints and app code in TypeScript/React.
- Static assets live in `public/`.
- Build output is generated into `dist/` (do not edit by hand).
- App configuration is in `vite.config.js`, `tsconfig.json`, and `tailwind.config.ts`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
- `pnpm dev`: start the local dev server via `./scripts/dev` (from repo root).
- `pnpm build`: run `turbo build` for all apps/packages.
- `pnpm test`: run `turbo test` (Vitest per workspace).
- `pnpm typecheck`: run `turbo typecheck`.
- `pnpm lint` / `pnpm lint:fix`: run Biome checks and auto-fixes.
- `pnpm storybook`: start Storybook (`apps/storybook`).
- `pnpm test:storybook`: run Storybook tests.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
- Indentation: 2 spaces (see `biome.jsonc`).
- Prefer semantic design tokens and Tailwind utilities from `packages/ui`.
- File naming: `kebab-case` for folders, `kebab-case.tsx` for components.
- Tests: `*.test.ts` or `*.test.tsx` next to the source module.

## Testing Guidelines
- Framework: Vitest (see each app/package `vitest.config.ts`).
- Name tests `*.test.ts(x)` and keep them close to the code they cover.
- Run all tests with `pnpm test`, or scope with `pnpm --filter <pkg> test`.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
- Commit messages follow Conventional Commits: `type(scope): subject`.
  - Example: `feat(sidebar): add collapse`.
- Commits must be GPG signed (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- Branch naming: `[username]/[feature-description]`.
- PRs should include: concise summary, linked issue (if any), test notes,
  and screenshots for UI changes.

## Security & Configuration Tips
- Use `./scripts/setup.sh` for local SSL/hosts setup (see `SETUP.md`).
- Keep `node` and `pnpm` aligned with `package.json` engines:
  `node >= 24.6.0`, `pnpm >= 10.6.4`.

### How to use skills
- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.
  3) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  4) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  5) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.
  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.

payload.text:
**Diagnosing TypeScript circular type inference**

The TS7022 error is likely caused by a circular type dependency where the route type and its component type reference each other during inference, especially with TS 5.8 and createFileRoute; this matches a known issue where placing the component function after the route definition and using explicit annotations helps break the cycle.

**Analyzing circular type inference causes**

The TS7022 error likely stems from circular type inference involving `Route` and `RouteComponent`, worsened by trailing slash mismatches or loader referencing `Route`. Adding explicit type annotations to `Route` or reorganizing component and route declarations may break the cycle, while the trailing slash difference likely doesn't cause TS7022 but can affect type inference.

settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

If a decision is necessary and cannot be discovered from local context, ask the user directly. However, in Default mode you should strongly prefer executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions.

payload.user_instructions:
<!-- BEGIN COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->
## Compound Codex Tool Mapping (Claude Compatibility)

This section maps Claude Code plugin tool references to Codex behavior.
Only this block is managed automatically.

Tool mapping:
- Read: use shell reads (cat/sed) or rg
- Write: create files via shell redirection or apply_patch
- Edit/MultiEdit: use apply_patch
- Bash: use shell_command
- Grep: use rg (fallback: grep)
- Glob: use rg --files or find
- LS: use ls via shell_command
- WebFetch/WebSearch: use curl or Context7 for library docs
- AskUserQuestion/Question: ask the user in chat
- Task/Subagent/Parallel: run sequentially in main thread; use multi_tool_use.parallel for tool calls
- TodoWrite/TodoRead: use file-based todos in todos/ with file-todos skill
- Skill: open the referenced SKILL.md and follow it
- ExitPlanMode: ignore
<!-- END COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->

--- project-doc ---

# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
- `apps/` contains product apps (e.g., `apps/org`, `apps/donor`, `apps/builder`, `apps/storybook`).
- `packages/` contains shared packages like `packages/ui`, `packages/utils`, `packages/tokens`, and config.
- Shared scripts live in `scripts/`, repo config in `config/`, and tooling in `turbo.json` + `biome.jsonc`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
- `pnpm dev` starts the local dev experience via `./scripts/dev`.
- `pnpm build` runs `turbo build` for all packages/apps.
- `pnpm test` runs `turbo test` (Vitest in each workspace).
- `pnpm typecheck` runs `turbo typecheck`.
- `pnpm lint` / `pnpm lint:fix` run Biome checks and auto-fixes.
- `pnpm storybook` starts Storybook (`apps/storybook`).
- `pnpm test:storybook` runs Storybook tests.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
- Indentation: 2 spaces (see `biome.jsonc`).
- Prefer semantic design tokens and Tailwind utility variables from `packages/ui` (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- File naming: use `kebab-case` for folders, `kebab-case.tsx` for components, and `*.test.ts(x)` for tests.
- Run Biome before pushing: `pnpm lint`.

## Testing Guidelines
- Testing framework: Vitest (see `vitest.config.ts` in apps/packages).
- Name tests as `*.test.ts` or `*.test.tsx` near the source module.
- Run all tests with `pnpm test`, or target a workspace with `pnpm --filter <pkg> test`.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
- Commit messages follow Conventional Commits: `type(scope): subject` (example: `feat(sidebar): add collapse`).
- Commits must be GPG signed (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- Branch naming: `[username]/[feature-description]`.
- PRs should include: concise summary, linked issue (if any), test notes, and screenshots for UI changes.

## Security & Configuration Tips
- Use the setup script for local SSL/hosts setup: `./scripts/setup.sh` (see `SETUP.md`).
- Keep `node` and `pnpm` aligned with `package.json` engines (`node >= 24.6.0`, `pnpm >= 10.6.4`).


# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
- Source lives in `src/`, with entrypoints and app code in TypeScript/React.
- Static assets live in `public/`.
- Build output is generated into `dist/` (do not edit by hand).
- App configuration is in `vite.config.js`, `tsconfig.json`, and `tailwind.config.ts`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
- `pnpm dev`: start the local dev server via `./scripts/dev` (from repo root).
- `pnpm build`: run `turbo build` for all apps/packages.
- `pnpm test`: run `turbo test` (Vitest per workspace).
- `pnpm typecheck`: run `turbo typecheck`.
- `pnpm lint` / `pnpm lint:fix`: run Biome checks and auto-fixes.
- `pnpm storybook`: start Storybook (`apps/storybook`).
- `pnpm test:storybook`: run Storybook tests.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
- Indentation: 2 spaces (see `biome.jsonc`).
- Prefer semantic design tokens and Tailwind utilities from `packages/ui`.
- File naming: `kebab-case` for folders, `kebab-case.tsx` for components.
- Tests: `*.test.ts` or `*.test.tsx` next to the source module.

## Testing Guidelines
- Framework: Vitest (see each app/package `vitest.config.ts`).
- Name tests `*.test.ts(x)` and keep them close to the code they cover.
- Run all tests with `pnpm test`, or scope with `pnpm --filter <pkg> test`.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
- Commit messages follow Conventional Commits: `type(scope): subject`.
  - Example: `feat(sidebar): add collapse`.
- Commits must be GPG signed (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- Branch naming: `[username]/[feature-description]`.
- PRs should include: concise summary, linked issue (if any), test notes,
  and screenshots for UI changes.

## Security & Configuration Tips
- Use `./scripts/setup.sh` for local SSL/hosts setup (see `SETUP.md`).
- Keep `node` and `pnpm` aligned with `package.json` engines:
  `node >= 24.6.0`, `pnpm >= 10.6.4`.

### How to use skills
- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.
  3) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  4) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  5) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.
  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.

payload.text:
**Analyzing route usage and trailing slash issues**

The index route likely has a trailing slash causing TypeScript inference issues, possibly due to mismatched route declarations or usage of `UpdateRoute.to` and `linkOptions` in loaders; next, I'll verify route usage timing and how the router plugin generates paths versus IDs.

**Clarifying trailing slash and circular type inference fixes**

Trailing slashes in `createFileRoute` are expected for index routes to keep typegen consistent, so it's best to keep them and adjust links to omit trailing slashes. The TS7022 error likely stems from circular type inference between `Route` and `RouteComponent`, which can be resolved by explicitly annotating the route component’s return type to break the cycle. I'll suggest verifying source file changes after builds and aligning usage with the new router plugin’s conventions.

settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

If a decision is necessary and cannot be discovered from local context, ask the user directly. However, in Default mode you should strongly prefer executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions.

payload.user_instructions:
<!-- BEGIN COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->
## Compound Codex Tool Mapping (Claude Compatibility)

This section maps Claude Code plugin tool references to Codex behavior.
Only this block is managed automatically.

Tool mapping:
- Read: use shell reads (cat/sed) or rg
- Write: create files via shell redirection or apply_patch
- Edit/MultiEdit: use apply_patch
- Bash: use shell_command
- Grep: use rg (fallback: grep)
- Glob: use rg --files or find
- LS: use ls via shell_command
- WebFetch/WebSearch: use curl or Context7 for library docs
- AskUserQuestion/Question: ask the user in chat
- Task/Subagent/Parallel: run sequentially in main thread; use multi_tool_use.parallel for tool calls
- TodoWrite/TodoRead: use file-based todos in todos/ with file-todos skill
- Skill: open the referenced SKILL.md and follow it
- ExitPlanMode: ignore
<!-- END COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->

--- project-doc ---

# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
- `apps/` contains product apps (e.g., `apps/org`, `apps/donor`, `apps/builder`, `apps/storybook`).
- `packages/` contains shared packages like `packages/ui`, `packages/utils`, `packages/tokens`, and config.
- Shared scripts live in `scripts/`, repo config in `config/`, and tooling in `turbo.json` + `biome.jsonc`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
- `pnpm dev` starts the local dev experience via `./scripts/dev`.
- `pnpm build` runs `turbo build` for all packages/apps.
- `pnpm test` runs `turbo test` (Vitest in each workspace).
- `pnpm typecheck` runs `turbo typecheck`.
- `pnpm lint` / `pnpm lint:fix` run Biome checks and auto-fixes.
- `pnpm storybook` starts Storybook (`apps/storybook`).
- `pnpm test:storybook` runs Storybook tests.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
- Indentation: 2 spaces (see `biome.jsonc`).
- Prefer semantic design tokens and Tailwind utility variables from `packages/ui` (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- File naming: use `kebab-case` for folders, `kebab-case.tsx` for components, and `*.test.ts(x)` for tests.
- Run Biome before pushing: `pnpm lint`.

## Testing Guidelines
- Testing framework: Vitest (see `vitest.config.ts` in apps/packages).
- Name tests as `*.test.ts` or `*.test.tsx` near the source module.
- Run all tests with `pnpm test`, or target a workspace with `pnpm --filter <pkg> test`.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
- Commit messages follow Conventional Commits: `type(scope): subject` (example: `feat(sidebar): add collapse`).
- Commits must be GPG signed (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- Branch naming: `[username]/[feature-description]`.
- PRs should include: concise summary, linked issue (if any), test notes, and screenshots for UI changes.

## Security & Configuration Tips
- Use the setup script for local SSL/hosts setup: `./scripts/setup.sh` (see `SETUP.md`).
- Keep `node` and `pnpm` aligned with `package.json` engines (`node >= 24.6.0`, `pnpm >= 10.6.4`).


# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
- Source lives in `src/`, with entrypoints and app code in TypeScript/React.
- Static assets live in `public/`.
- Build output is generated into `dist/` (do not edit by hand).

- Prefer semantic design tokens and Tailwind utilities from `packages/ui`.
- File naming: `kebab-case` for folders, `kebab-case.tsx` for components.
- Tests: `*.test.ts` or `*.test.tsx` next to the source module.

## Testing Guidelines
- Framework: Vitest (see each app/package `vitest.config.ts`).
- Name tests `*.test.ts(x)` and keep them close to the code they cover.
- Run all tests with `pnpm test`, or scope with `pnpm --filter <pkg> test`.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
- Commit messages follow Conventional Commits: `type(scope): subject`.

- Commits must be GPG signed (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- Branch naming: `[username]/[feature-description]`.
- PRs should include: concise summary, linked issue (if any), test notes,
  and screenshots for UI changes.

### How to use skills
- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.
  3) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  4) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  5) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.
  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.

payload.text:
**Clarifying routeTree.gen changes and TS7022 cause**

Only routeTree.gen regenerates on build, not source files; the trailing slash in the index source remains unchanged. The TS7022 error likely stems from a circular type inference involving `Route` usage in the component; breaking this by explicitly typing `RouteComponent` or avoiding direct `Route` references via hooks like `useLoaderData` can resolve the cycle.

**Confirming routeTree.gen change and proposing type fixes**

Only routeTree.gen updates on build, consistent with `git status` showing just `src/routeTree.gen.ts`. The trailing slash in the index route ID is expected in v1.121.39. The TS7022 error likely stems from circular inference due to `RouteComponent` referencing `Route`; it can be fixed by typing `RouteComponent`’s return, avoiding direct `Route` use via `useLoaderData` and `useParams` with `from`, or leveraging `getRouteApi` for typed hooks.

settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

If a decision is necessary and cannot be discovered from local context, ask the user directly. However, in Default mode you should strongly prefer executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions.

payload.user_instructions:
<!-- BEGIN COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->
## Compound Codex Tool Mapping (Claude Compatibility)

This section maps Claude Code plugin tool references to Codex behavior.
Only this block is managed automatically.

Tool mapping:
- Read: use shell reads (cat/sed) or rg
- Write: create files via shell redirection or apply_patch
- Edit/MultiEdit: use apply_patch
- Bash: use shell_command
- Grep: use rg (fallback: grep)
- Glob: use rg --files or find
- LS: use ls via shell_command
- WebFetch/WebSearch: use curl or Context7 for library docs
- AskUserQuestion/Question: ask the user in chat
- Task/Subagent/Parallel: run sequentially in main thread; use multi_tool_use.parallel for tool calls
- TodoWrite/TodoRead: use file-based todos in todos/ with file-todos skill
- Skill: open the referenced SKILL.md and follow it
- ExitPlanMode: ignore
<!-- END COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->

--- project-doc ---

# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
- `apps/` contains product apps (e.g., `apps/org`, `apps/donor`, `apps/builder`, `apps/storybook`).
- `packages/` contains shared packages like `packages/ui`, `packages/utils`, `packages/tokens`, and config.
- Shared scripts live in `scripts/`, repo config in `config/`, and tooling in `turbo.json` + `biome.jsonc`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
- `pnpm dev` starts the local dev experience via `./scripts/dev`.
- `pnpm build` runs `turbo build` for all packages/apps.
- `pnpm test` runs `turbo test` (Vitest in each workspace).
- `pnpm typecheck` runs `turbo typecheck`.
- `pnpm lint` / `pnpm lint:fix` run Biome checks and auto-fixes.
- `pnpm storybook` starts Storybook (`apps/storybook`).
- `pnpm test:storybook` runs Storybook tests.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
- Indentation: 2 spaces (see `biome.jsonc`).
- Prefer semantic design tokens and Tailwind utility variables from `packages/ui` (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- File naming: use `kebab-case` for folders, `kebab-case.tsx` for components, and `*.test.ts(x)` for tests.
- Run Biome before pushing: `pnpm lint`.

## Testing Guidelines
- Testing framework: Vitest (see `vitest.config.ts` in apps/packages).
- Name tests as `*.test.ts` or `*.test.tsx` near the source module.
- Run all tests with `pnpm test`, or target a workspace with `pnpm --filter <pkg> test`.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
- Commit messages follow Conventional Commits: `type(scope): subject` (example: `feat(sidebar): add collapse`).
- Commits must be GPG signed (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- Branch naming: `[username]/[feature-description]`.
- PRs should include: concise summary, linked issue (if any), test notes, and screenshots for UI changes.

## Security & Configuration Tips
- Use the setup script for local SSL/hosts setup: `./scripts/setup.sh` (see `SETUP.md`).
- Keep `node` and `pnpm` aligned with `package.json` engines (`node >= 24.6.0`, `pnpm >= 10.6.4`).


# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
- Source lives in `src/`, with entrypoints and app code in TypeScript/React.
- Static assets live in `public/`.
- Build output is generated into `dist/` (do not edit by hand).
- App configuration is in `vite.config.js`, `tsconfig.json`, and `tailwind.config.ts`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
- `pnpm dev`: start the local dev server via `./scripts/dev` (from repo root).
- `pnpm build`: run `turbo build` for all apps/packages.
- `pnpm test`: run `turbo test` (Vitest per workspace).
- `pnpm typecheck`: run `turbo typecheck`.
- `pnpm lint` / `pnpm lint:fix`: run Biome checks and auto-fixes.
- `pnpm storybook`: start Storybook (`apps/storybook`).
- `pnpm test:storybook`: run Storybook tests.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
- Indentation: 2 spaces (see `biome.jsonc`).
- Prefer semantic design tokens and Tailwind utilities from `packages/ui`.
- File naming: `kebab-case` for folders, `kebab-case.tsx` for components.
- Tests: `*.test.ts` or `*.test.tsx` next to the source module.

## Testing Guidelines
- Framework: Vitest (see each app/package `vitest.config.ts`).
- Name tests `*.test.ts(x)` and keep them close to the code they cover.
- Run all tests with `pnpm test`, or scope with `pnpm --filter <pkg> test`.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
- Commit messages follow Conventional Commits: `type(scope): subject`.
  - Example: `feat(sidebar): add collapse`.
- Commits must be GPG signed (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- Branch naming: `[username]/[feature-description]`.
- PRs should include: concise summary, linked issue (if any), test notes,
  and screenshots for UI changes.

## Security & Configuration Tips
- Use `./scripts/setup.sh` for local SSL/hosts setup (see `SETUP.md`).
- Keep `node` and `pnpm` aligned with `package.json` engines:
  `node >= 24.6.0`, `pnpm >= 10.6.4`.

### How to use skills
- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.
  3) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  4) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  5) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.
  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.

settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

If a decision is necessary and cannot be discovered from local context, ask the user directly. However, in Default mode you should strongly prefer executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions.

payload.user_instructions:
<!-- BEGIN COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->
## Compound Codex Tool Mapping (Claude Compatibility)

This section maps Claude Code plugin tool references to Codex behavior.
Only this block is managed automatically.

Tool mapping:
- Read: use shell reads (cat/sed) or rg
- Write: create files via shell redirection or apply_patch
- Edit/MultiEdit: use apply_patch
- Bash: use shell_command
- Grep: use rg (fallback: grep)
- Glob: use rg --files or find
- LS: use ls via shell_command
- WebFetch/WebSearch: use curl or Context7 for library docs
- AskUserQuestion/Question: ask the user in chat
- Task/Subagent/Parallel: run sequentially in main thread; use multi_tool_use.parallel for tool calls
- TodoWrite/TodoRead: use file-based todos in todos/ with file-todos skill
- Skill: open the referenced SKILL.md and follow it
- ExitPlanMode: ignore
<!-- END COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->

--- project-doc ---

# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
- `apps/` contains product apps (e.g., `apps/org`, `apps/donor`, `apps/builder`, `apps/storybook`).
- `packages/` contains shared packages like `packages/ui`, `packages/utils`, `packages/tokens`, and config.
- Shared scripts live in `scripts/`, repo config in `config/`, and tooling in `turbo.json` + `biome.jsonc`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
- `pnpm dev` starts the local dev experience via `./scripts/dev`.
- `pnpm build` runs `turbo build` for all packages/apps.
- `pnpm test` runs `turbo test` (Vitest in each workspace).
- `pnpm typecheck` runs `turbo typecheck`.
- `pnpm lint` / `pnpm lint:fix` run Biome checks and auto-fixes.
- `pnpm storybook` starts Storybook (`apps/storybook`).
- `pnpm test:storybook` runs Storybook tests.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
- Indentation: 2 spaces (see `biome.jsonc`).
- Prefer semantic design tokens and Tailwind utility variables from `packages/ui` (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- File naming: use `kebab-case` for folders, `kebab-case.tsx` for components, and `*.test.ts(x)` for tests.
- Run Biome before pushing: `pnpm lint`.

## Testing Guidelines
- Testing framework: Vitest (see `vitest.config.ts` in apps/packages).
- Name tests as `*.test.ts` or `*.test.tsx` near the source module.
- Run all tests with `pnpm test`, or target a workspace with `pnpm --filter <pkg> test`.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
- Commit messages follow Conventional Commits: `type(scope): subject` (example: `feat(sidebar): add collapse`).
- Commits must be GPG signed (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- Branch naming: `[username]/[feature-description]`.
- PRs should include: concise summary, linked issue (if any), test notes, and screenshots for UI changes.

## Security & Configuration Tips
- Use the setup script for local SSL/hosts setup: `./scripts/setup.sh` (see `SETUP.md`).
- Keep `node` and `pnpm` aligned with `package.json` engines (`node >= 24.6.0`, `pnpm >= 10.6.4`).


# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
- Source lives in `src/`, with entrypoints and app code in TypeScript/React.
- Static assets live in `public/`.
- Build output is generated into `dist/` (do not edit by hand).
- App configuration is in `vite.config.js`, `tsconfig.json`, and `tailwind.config.ts`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
- `pnpm dev`: start the local dev server via `./scripts/dev` (from repo root).
- `pnpm build`: run `turbo build` for all apps/packages.
- `pnpm test`: run `turbo test` (Vitest per workspace).
- `pnpm typecheck`: run `turbo typecheck`.
- `pnpm lint` / `pnpm lint:fix`: run Biome checks and auto-fixes.
- `pnpm storybook`: start Storybook (`apps/storybook`).
- `pnpm test:storybook`: run Storybook tests.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
- Indentation: 2 spaces (see `biome.jsonc`).
- Prefer semantic design tokens and Tailwind utilities from `packages/ui`.
- File naming: `kebab-case` for folders, `kebab-case.tsx` for components.
- Tests: `*.test.ts` or `*.test.tsx` next to the source module.

## Testing Guidelines
- Framework: Vitest (see each app/package `vitest.config.ts`).
- Name tests `*.test.ts(x)` and keep them close to the code they cover.
- Run all tests with `pnpm test`, or scope with `pnpm --filter <pkg> test`.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
- Commit messages follow Conventional Commits: `type(scope): subject`.
  - Example: `feat(sidebar): add collapse`.
- Commits must be GPG signed (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- Branch naming: `[username]/[feature-description]`.
- PRs should include: concise summary, linked issue (if any), test notes,
  and screenshots for UI changes.

## Security & Configuration Tips
- Use `./scripts/setup.sh` for local SSL/hosts setup (see `SETUP.md`).
- Keep `node` and `pnpm` aligned with `package.json` engines:
  `node >= 24.6.0`, `pnpm >= 10.6.4`.

### How to use skills
- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.
  3) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  4) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  5) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.
  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.

payload.message:
I’d recommend **option 3 (`getRouteApi`)**.

**Why option 3 is best long‑term**
- **No type‑inference cycle**: you don’t reference `Route` inside the component, so TS7022 goes away cleanly.
- **Stays strongly typed**: you keep full route‑specific typing without sprinkling `from` everywhere.
- **Scales to shared components**: you can pass the route API down or import it where needed.

**Tradeoffs**

**Option 1 (annotate return type)**
- Pros: smallest diff, fast.
- Cons: it’s a **band‑aid**. The cycle still exists; TS just stops complaining. Future refactors can re‑surface weird inference or any‑leaks.
- Risk: if the router types change again, the implicit cycle can bite you elsewhere.

**Option 3 (`getRouteApi`)**
- Pros: resolves the real cycle and aligns with TanStack’s recommended pattern for typed hooks in non‑route modules.
- Cons: slightly more boilerplate (one `routeApi` const).
- Best for stability in upgraded versions.

If you want, I’ll implement option 3 for `src/routes/token-auth/donation.$token/index.tsx`.

content.text:
I’d recommend **option 3 (`getRouteApi`)**.

**Why option 3 is best long‑term**
- **No type‑inference cycle**: you don’t reference `Route` inside the component, so TS7022 goes away cleanly.
- **Stays strongly typed**: you keep full route‑specific typing without sprinkling `from` everywhere.
- **Scales to shared components**: you can pass the route API down or import it where needed.

**Tradeoffs**

**Option 1 (annotate return type)**
- Pros: smallest diff, fast.
- Cons: it’s a **band‑aid**. The cycle still exists; TS just stops complaining. Future refactors can re‑surface weird inference or any‑leaks.
- Risk: if the router types change again, the implicit cycle can bite you elsewhere.

**Option 3 (`getRouteApi`)**
- Pros: resolves the real cycle and aligns with TanStack’s recommended pattern for typed hooks in non‑route modules.
- Cons: slightly more boilerplate (one `routeApi` const).
- Best for stability in upgraded versions.

If you want, I’ll implement option 3 for `src/routes/token-auth/donation.$token/index.tsx`.

# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

If a decision is necessary and cannot be discovered from local context, ask the user directly. However, in Default mode you should strongly prefer executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions.

<!-- BEGIN COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->
## Compound Codex Tool Mapping (Claude Compatibility)

This section maps Claude Code plugin tool references to Codex behavior.
Only this block is managed automatically.

Tool mapping:
- Read: use shell reads (cat/sed) or rg
- Write: create files via shell redirection or apply_patch
- Edit/MultiEdit: use apply_patch
- Bash: use shell_command
- Grep: use rg (fallback: grep)
- Glob: use rg --files or find
- LS: use ls via shell_command
- WebFetch/WebSearch: use curl or Context7 for library docs
- AskUserQuestion/Question: ask the user in chat
- Task/Subagent/Parallel: run sequentially in main thread; use multi_tool_use.parallel for tool calls
- TodoWrite/TodoRead: use file-based todos in todos/ with file-todos skill
- Skill: open the referenced SKILL.md and follow it
- ExitPlanMode: ignore
<!-- END COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->

# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
- `apps/` contains product apps (e.g., `apps/org`, `apps/donor`, `apps/builder`, `apps/storybook`).
- `packages/` contains shared packages like `packages/ui`, `packages/utils`, `packages/tokens`, and config.
- Shared scripts live in `scripts/`, repo config in `config/`, and tooling in `turbo.json` + `biome.jsonc`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
- `pnpm dev` starts the local dev experience via `./scripts/dev`.
- `pnpm build` runs `turbo build` for all packages/apps.
- `pnpm test` runs `turbo test` (Vitest in each workspace).
- `pnpm typecheck` runs `turbo typecheck`.
- `pnpm lint` / `pnpm lint:fix` run Biome checks and auto-fixes.
- `pnpm storybook` starts Storybook (`apps/storybook`).
- `pnpm test:storybook` runs Storybook tests.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
- Indentation: 2 spaces (see `biome.jsonc`).
- Prefer semantic design tokens and Tailwind utility variables from `packages/ui` (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- File naming: use `kebab-case` for folders, `kebab-case.tsx` for components, and `*.test.ts(x)` for tests.
- Run Biome before pushing: `pnpm lint`.

## Testing Guidelines
- Testing framework: Vitest (see `vitest.config.ts` in apps/packages).
- Name tests as `*.test.ts` or `*.test.tsx` near the source module.
- Run all tests with `pnpm test`, or target a workspace with `pnpm --filter <pkg> test`.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
- Commit messages follow Conventional Commits: `type(scope): subject` (example: `feat(sidebar): add collapse`).
- Commits must be GPG signed (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- Branch naming: `[username]/[feature-description]`.
- PRs should include: concise summary, linked issue (if any), test notes, and screenshots for UI changes.

## Security & Configuration Tips
- Use the setup script for local SSL/hosts setup: `./scripts/setup.sh` (see `SETUP.md`).
- Keep `node` and `pnpm` aligned with `package.json` engines (`node >= 24.6.0`, `pnpm >= 10.6.4`).


# Repository Guidelines

- Source lives in `src/`, with entrypoints and app code in TypeScript/React.
- Static assets live in `public/`.
- Build output is generated into `dist/` (do not edit by hand).

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
- `pnpm dev`: start the local dev server via `./scripts/dev` (from repo root).
- `pnpm build`: run `turbo build` for all apps/packages.

- `pnpm storybook`: start Storybook (`apps/storybook`).
- `pnpm test:storybook`: run Storybook tests.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
- Indentation: 2 spaces (see `biome.jsonc`).
- Prefer semantic design tokens and Tailwind utilities from `packages/ui`.
- File naming: `kebab-case` for folders, `kebab-case.tsx` for components.
- Tests: `*.test.ts` or `*.test.tsx` next to the source module.

## Testing Guidelines
- Framework: Vitest (see each app/package `vitest.config.ts`).
- Name tests `*.test.ts(x)` and keep them close to the code they cover.
- Run all tests with `pnpm test`, or scope with `pnpm --filter <pkg> test`.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
- Commit messages follow Conventional Commits: `type(scope): subject`.
  - Example: `feat(sidebar): add collapse`.
- Commits must be GPG signed (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- Branch naming: `[username]/[feature-description]`.
- PRs should include: concise summary, linked issue (if any), test notes,
  and screenshots for UI changes.

## Security & Configuration Tips
- Use `./scripts/setup.sh` for local SSL/hosts setup (see `SETUP.md`).
- Keep `node` and `pnpm` aligned with `package.json` engines:
  `node >= 24.6.0`, `pnpm >= 10.6.4`.

### How to use skills
- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.
  3) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  4) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  5) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.
  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.

settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

If a decision is necessary and cannot be discovered from local context, ask the user directly. However, in Default mode you should strongly prefer executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions.

payload.user_instructions:
<!-- BEGIN COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->
## Compound Codex Tool Mapping (Claude Compatibility)

This section maps Claude Code plugin tool references to Codex behavior.
Only this block is managed automatically.

Tool mapping:
- Read: use shell reads (cat/sed) or rg
- Write: create files via shell redirection or apply_patch
- Edit/MultiEdit: use apply_patch
- Bash: use shell_command
- Grep: use rg (fallback: grep)
- Glob: use rg --files or find
- LS: use ls via shell_command
- WebFetch/WebSearch: use curl or Context7 for library docs
- AskUserQuestion/Question: ask the user in chat
- Task/Subagent/Parallel: run sequentially in main thread; use multi_tool_use.parallel for tool calls
- TodoWrite/TodoRead: use file-based todos in todos/ with file-todos skill
- Skill: open the referenced SKILL.md and follow it
- ExitPlanMode: ignore
<!-- END COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->

--- project-doc ---

# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
- `apps/` contains product apps (e.g., `apps/org`, `apps/donor`, `apps/builder`, `apps/storybook`).
- `packages/` contains shared packages like `packages/ui`, `packages/utils`, `packages/tokens`, and config.
- Shared scripts live in `scripts/`, repo config in `config/`, and tooling in `turbo.json` + `biome.jsonc`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
- `pnpm dev` starts the local dev experience via `./scripts/dev`.
- `pnpm build` runs `turbo build` for all packages/apps.
- `pnpm test` runs `turbo test` (Vitest in each workspace).
- `pnpm typecheck` runs `turbo typecheck`.
- `pnpm lint` / `pnpm lint:fix` run Biome checks and auto-fixes.
- `pnpm storybook` starts Storybook (`apps/storybook`).
- `pnpm test:storybook` runs Storybook tests.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
- Indentation: 2 spaces (see `biome.jsonc`).
- Prefer semantic design tokens and Tailwind utility variables from `packages/ui` (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- File naming: use `kebab-case` for folders, `kebab-case.tsx` for components, and `*.test.ts(x)` for tests.
- Run Biome before pushing: `pnpm lint`.

## Testing Guidelines
- Testing framework: Vitest (see `vitest.config.ts` in apps/packages).
- Name tests as `*.test.ts` or `*.test.tsx` near the source module.
- Run all tests with `pnpm test`, or target a workspace with `pnpm --filter <pkg> test`.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
- Commit messages follow Conventional Commits: `type(scope): subject` (example: `feat(sidebar): add collapse`).
- Commits must be GPG signed (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- Branch naming: `[username]/[feature-description]`.
- PRs should include: concise summary, linked issue (if any), test notes, and screenshots for UI changes.

## Security & Configuration Tips
- Use the setup script for local SSL/hosts setup: `./scripts/setup.sh` (see `SETUP.md`).
- Keep `node` and `pnpm` aligned with `package.json` engines (`node >= 24.6.0`, `pnpm >= 10.6.4`).


# Repository Guidelines

- Source lives in `src/`, with entrypoints and app code in TypeScript/React.

- Prefer semantic design tokens and Tailwind utilities from `packages/ui`.
- File naming: `kebab-case` for folders, `kebab-case.tsx` for components.
- Tests: `*.test.ts` or `*.test.tsx` next to the source module.

## Testing Guidelines
- Framework: Vitest (see each app/package `vitest.config.ts`).
- Name tests `*.test.ts(x)` and keep them close to the code they cover.
- Run all tests with `pnpm test`, or scope with `pnpm --filter <pkg> test`.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
- Commit messages follow Conventional Commits: `type(scope): subject`.

- Branch naming: `[username]/[feature-description]`.

### How to use skills
- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.
  3) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  4) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  5) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.
  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.

settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

If a decision is necessary and cannot be discovered from local context, ask the user directly. However, in Default mode you should strongly prefer executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions.

payload.user_instructions:
<!-- BEGIN COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->
## Compound Codex Tool Mapping (Claude Compatibility)

This section maps Claude Code plugin tool references to Codex behavior.
Only this block is managed automatically.

Tool mapping:
- Read: use shell reads (cat/sed) or rg
- Write: create files via shell redirection or apply_patch
- Edit/MultiEdit: use apply_patch
- Bash: use shell_command
- Grep: use rg (fallback: grep)
- Glob: use rg --files or find
- LS: use ls via shell_command
- WebFetch/WebSearch: use curl or Context7 for library docs
- AskUserQuestion/Question: ask the user in chat
- Task/Subagent/Parallel: run sequentially in main thread; use multi_tool_use.parallel for tool calls
- TodoWrite/TodoRead: use file-based todos in todos/ with file-todos skill
- Skill: open the referenced SKILL.md and follow it
- ExitPlanMode: ignore
<!-- END COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->

--- project-doc ---

# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
- `apps/` contains product apps (e.g., `apps/org`, `apps/donor`, `apps/builder`, `apps/storybook`).
- `packages/` contains shared packages like `packages/ui`, `packages/utils`, `packages/tokens`, and config.
- Shared scripts live in `scripts/`, repo config in `config/`, and tooling in `turbo.json` + `biome.jsonc`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
- `pnpm dev` starts the local dev experience via `./scripts/dev`.
- `pnpm build` runs `turbo build` for all packages/apps.
- `pnpm test` runs `turbo test` (Vitest in each workspace).
- `pnpm typecheck` runs `turbo typecheck`.
- `pnpm lint` / `pnpm lint:fix` run Biome checks and auto-fixes.
- `pnpm storybook` starts Storybook (`apps/storybook`).
- `pnpm test:storybook` runs Storybook tests.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
- Indentation: 2 spaces (see `biome.jsonc`).
- Prefer semantic design tokens and Tailwind utility variables from `packages/ui` (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- File naming: use `kebab-case` for folders, `kebab-case.tsx` for components, and `*.test.ts(x)` for tests.
- Run Biome before pushing: `pnpm lint`.

## Testing Guidelines
- Testing framework: Vitest (see `vitest.config.ts` in apps/packages).
- Name tests as `*.test.ts` or `*.test.tsx` near the source module.
- Run all tests with `pnpm test`, or target a workspace with `pnpm --filter <pkg> test`.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
- Commit messages follow Conventional Commits: `type(scope): subject` (example: `feat(sidebar): add collapse`).
- Commits must be GPG signed (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- Branch naming: `[username]/[feature-description]`.
- PRs should include: concise summary, linked issue (if any), test notes, and screenshots for UI changes.

## Security & Configuration Tips
- Use the setup script for local SSL/hosts setup: `./scripts/setup.sh` (see `SETUP.md`).
- Keep `node` and `pnpm` aligned with `package.json` engines (`node >= 24.6.0`, `pnpm >= 10.6.4`).


# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
- Source lives in `src/`, with entrypoints and app code in TypeScript/React.
- Static assets live in `public/`.
- Build output is generated into `dist/` (do not edit by hand).
- App configuration is in `vite.config.js`, `tsconfig.json`, and `tailwind.config.ts`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
- `pnpm dev`: start the local dev server via `./scripts/dev` (from repo root).

- `pnpm storybook`: start Storybook (`apps/storybook`).
- `pnpm test:storybook`: run Storybook tests.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
- Indentation: 2 spaces (see `biome.jsonc`).
- Prefer semantic design tokens and Tailwind utilities from `packages/ui`.
- File naming: `kebab-case` for folders, `kebab-case.tsx` for components.
- Tests: `*.test.ts` or `*.test.tsx` next to the source module.

## Testing Guidelines
- Framework: Vitest (see each app/package `vitest.config.ts`).
- Name tests `*.test.ts(x)` and keep them close to the code they cover.
- Run all tests with `pnpm test`, or scope with `pnpm --filter <pkg> test`.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
- Commit messages follow Conventional Commits: `type(scope): subject`.
  - Example: `feat(sidebar): add collapse`.
- Commits must be GPG signed (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- Branch naming: `[username]/[feature-description]`.
- PRs should include: concise summary, linked issue (if any), test notes,
  and screenshots for UI changes.

## Security & Configuration Tips
- Use `./scripts/setup.sh` for local SSL/hosts setup (see `SETUP.md`).
- Keep `node` and `pnpm` aligned with `package.json` engines:
  `node >= 24.6.0`, `pnpm >= 10.6.4`.

### How to use skills
- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.
  3) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  4) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  5) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.
  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.
